critic_raw:
Using clicks as labels for article recommendation introduces popularity bias by construction. Articles that were displayed more frequently in the historical system accumulated more clicks regardless of their semantic relevance. A model trained to predict clicks will learn to recommend popular articles — exactly what the cosine similarity baseline also does, since popular articles appear more in interaction logs. The experiment cannot distinguish whether any CTR improvement is due to better semantic modeling or better modeling of past display policy.

The stratified random split does not use user as the split unit. The same user's interactions span training and test, allowing the model to learn user-specific behavior patterns (specific interests, click tendencies) that generalize within-user but not across users. This is user leakage, not temporal leakage.

CTR from offline historical logs under a different recommendation policy is not the same as CTR under the proposed policy. This is the standard off-policy evaluation problem for recommendation systems. The historical logs were generated by the keyword cosine similarity system — articles the keyword system never showed have zero logged clicks even if the transformer would rank them highly, creating a counterfactual gap that CTR cannot bridge without correction.

The transformer is fine-tuned on abstracts, not on user interaction patterns, but the evaluation metric is user CTR. The model must bridge from semantic similarity to user engagement without any explicit engagement training signal — this connection is assumed but never justified. If users click on papers for reasons other than abstract-level semantic similarity (e.g., author reputation, journal prestige, title framing), the transformer's abstract-level signal will not predict CTR accurately.
all_issues_raised: Click labels encode popularity bias from the historical recommendation policy rather than semantic relevance — training on clicks causes both the transformer and the cosine similarity model to learn display-frequency patterns, making any CTR difference uninterpretable as evidence of semantic modeling quality.

The stratified random split does not hold out users, allowing the model to learn user-specific click tendencies from training interactions and apply them at test time, inflating apparent recommendation quality beyond what would be observed for new users.

Offline CTR estimation under a historical policy generated by a different recommendation system is subject to off-policy evaluation bias — articles never shown by the keyword system have zero observed CTR regardless of their true relevance to users.

The transformer is fine-tuned on abstract text but evaluated on user CTR, with no justification for why abstract-level semantic similarity predicts user engagement — if clicks are driven by author reputation or journal prestige rather than abstract content, the entire modeling premise fails.
all_issues_adjudicated: Click labels encode popularity bias from the historical recommendation policy rather than semantic relevance — training on clicks causes both the transformer and the cosine similarity model to learn display-frequency patterns, making any CTR difference uninterpretable as evidence of semantic modeling quality.

The stratified random split does not hold out users, allowing the model to learn user-specific click tendencies from training interactions and apply them at test time, inflating apparent recommendation quality beyond what would be observed for new users.

Offline CTR estimation under a historical policy generated by a different recommendation system is subject to off-policy evaluation bias — articles never shown by the keyword system have zero observed CTR regardless of their true relevance to users.

The transformer is fine-tuned on abstract text but evaluated on user CTR, with no justification for why abstract-level semantic similarity predicts user engagement — if clicks are driven by author reputation or journal prestige rather than abstract content, the entire modeling premise fails.
